methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series F → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.3x) to $169M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Helion
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series F), post-money $15.5B

data.narrative:
CarbonCure Technologies is a Series F climate and energy company that has raised $169M to date. The valuation universe for this stage and sector includes 1 public comparable (Linzatech, ticker LNZA) and 8 private peers spanning similar frontier-energy subsectors, including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen. The narrow peer set at identical stage and valuation tier proved insufficient (fewer than 3 datapoints) to support a reliable peer-median multiple approach.

The analysis applies a stage-progression methodology, which is applicable given the Series F maturity and capital raised. Using the $169M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 1.3x step-up multiplier from Series F to later stage, this method yields $220M. A bracketing range around this single triangulation point—anchored at the median of $220M with a ±15% buffer—produces a low estimate of $187M and a high estimate of $253M.

The valuation analysis supports a range of $187M–$253M, centered on $220M. Key uncertainties include the limited peer comparability at this specific stage within the sector; a broader peer-median analysis would require relaxing stage or subsector filters, introducing additional noise. The stage-progression result should be stress-tested against forward capital requirements, unit economics in carbon-utilization deployment, and margin trajectory relative to public-market expectations for climate-tech exits.
